headline: NUSOJ Report: Disinformation and Clan-Based Manipulation Pose Serious Challenge to Mogadishu's First Local Elections in Decades
summary: According to a report by the National Union of Somali Journalists (NUSOJ) released January 14, 2026, and covering the monitoring period November 1–December 30, 2025, disinformation and misinformation constituted one of the most serious challenges to the Banadir local council elections — Mogadishu's first direct local elections since 1969, with 1,604 candidates competing for 390 seats across 16 districts. According to NUSOJ, online falsehoods were moderate to high before voting, declined slightly on election day, but surged sharply afterward, fueling polarization and undermining public confidence; key tactics included exploitation of clan identity to discredit the vote without evidence, amplified by algorithms and accounts outside Somalia. Despite the disinformation environment, the elections were assessed as peaceful and orderly overall, though marred by state media bias alongside the information disorder.
extracted_claims: Various clan-based manipulation and disinformation targeting Mogadishu local elections

summary: On March 18, al-Shabaab militants planted a roadside bomb near the Ceel-gaabta junction in Xamar-Jajab district, targeting President Hassan Sheikh Mohamud's motorcade as he traveled to the airport. The bomb killed at least 10 people including seven presidential guards and a journalist (Radio Risaala's Mohamed Abukar Dabaashe), and injured 20 others. The president escaped unharmed and continued to the frontlines. Al-Shabaab immediately claimed responsibility via Shahada News Agency and Telegram, framing the attack as an operation to eliminate the 'apostate president' — the first direct assassination attempt on Mohamud since 2014. The attack drew international condemnation from the Arab League, OIC, and UN Secretary-General.

The White Army is documented as "an amalgamation of community militias" rather than SPLA-IO-commanded forces. The false attribution narrative was amplified through state media and SSPDF military statements, while UPDF statements equated "SPLA-IO, also known as the White Army" — a factually incorrect conflation. headline: Anti-Turkey disinformation claims Turkish military in Somalia is "siphoning natural resources"; leaked MIT report documents the campaign

The anti-Turkey narratives, suspected as Gulf-rival-linked, exploit Somalia's fragile media ecosystem where verification capacity is low. Turkey responded by launching TRT Somali in March 2025 as a Somali-language counter-narrative channel.
escalation_outcome: Undermining stabilisation partnerships through narrative attacks

headline: Trump's first Somalia airstrike kills senior ISIS-Somalia commander and 46 fighters
summary: At President Trump's direction, AFRICOM conducted its first airstrike of his second term on February 1, targeting ISIS-Somalia operatives in the Golis Mountains near Bossaso. Ten strikes targeted cave systems and killed 46 fighters including the deputy to ISIS-Somalia leader Abdul Qadir Mumin, the head of the group's training, and a key recruiter/financier. Secretary of Defense Hegseth personally confirmed the strike, framing it as part of a broader counterterrorism doctrine. This marked the start of a dramatic escalation in US airstrikes that would eventually reach 109+ in 2025 and 26 in January 2026 alone.

headline: Livestock vaccine conspiracy: Kalonzo Musyoka claims government plan uses "gene-modifying drugs" on cattle as "foreign agenda"

The narrative spread via the "My Cow, My Choice" hashtag on social media, with claims that Bill Gates was involved and vaccines would alter cattle DNA to reduce methane emissions. Kenya's Agriculture CS Dr. Andrew Karanja responded that vaccines are manufactured domestically by KEVEVAPI. Africa Check identified this as one of five key disinformation trends unfolding in Kenya in 2025. The false claims directly threatened uptake of a programme combating diseases that cost Kenya billions in livestock losses annually.
escalation_outcome: Farmer resistance to vaccination programme; public health threat to livestock sector

summary: Military narratives framing women as "rewards of war" and sexual violence as military benefit. AI-generated and real content conflated to normalize violence.

headline: Political gangs attack church congregation over "Wantam" narrative in Kasarani
summary: NAIROBI-047-001: "A gang group attack[ed] a congregation within a Church in Kasarani because the politicians who were present in that church were going to sell the 'Wantam' narrative. Both the politicians affiliated to the two narratives now have gang groups whom they move with to protect them during political rallies. There have also been threats issued where some politicians warn their colleagues that they are not allowed to campaign in some regions."

headline: False claim Kenya government planning to implant microchips in newborn babies via digital ID programme
summary: A previously debunked 2023 claim resurfaced that Kenya's digital ID programme (Maisha Namba) would implant microchips in newborns. The viral content spliced together unrelated clips of President Ruto and Bill Gates to suggest sinister intentions. Separately, a fabricated quote attributed to Health CS Susan Nakhumicha claimed "DNA tests for newborns are now mandatory nationwide" — Africa Check confirmed no such statement was made. These health conspiracy narratives exploit distrust of digital governance and foreign philanthropy, undermining public health programmes and digital identity rollout.
escalation_outcome: Undermining digital ID and public health programme uptake

headline: Africa Center documents fourfold surge in disinformation: Russia leads with 80 campaigns in 22 countries; Kenya highest domestic rate at >60%
summary: Africa Center for Strategic Studies documented a fourfold increase in disinformation campaigns across Africa since 2022. Nearly 60% of campaigns are foreign state-sponsored, with Russia (~40% of content, 80 campaigns, 22 countries), China (5 multi-regional campaigns via CCP United Front), UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Qatar as primary sponsors. Kenya has the highest percentage of domestically originating campaigns (>60%) with 9 documented campaigns total. East Africa has the second-highest number of documented campaigns continent-wide. ISS African Futures documented how Russia, China, and Turkey all offer training to African reporters as part of influence strategies. Two well-known Russian-linked African disinformation spreaders alone have 28 million social media followers.
escalation_outcome: Documented systemic threat to East African information ecosystems

headline: Pravda network injects 3.7M articles into Wikipedia and AI chatbots, embedding anti-Western narratives into East African information supply chain
summary: DFRLab/CheckFirst investigation documented the Russian Pravda network publishing 3.7 million articles across 140+ subdomains in 83+ countries, repurposing Russian state media content. 1,900+ Pravda hyperlinks were embedded in 44 Wikipedia language versions including Swahili, contaminating AI language models (ChatGPT, Gemini, Copilot, Perplexity) that use Wikipedia as primary source material. Key narratives include: "NATO expansion caused the Ukraine war" (believed by >70% of African respondents), "Western sanctions cause food/energy crises" (not Russia's invasion), and anti-Western geopolitical framing. For East Africa, where AI tools are increasingly used for news consumption, this represents systematic poisoning of the information supply chain — users receive Kremlin-aligned narratives through seemingly neutral AI assistants.
escalation_outcome: Systemic contamination of AI-mediated information

headline: CGTN/Xinhua push "debt trap is a Western myth" narrative dismissing Kenya's $1B/year SGR debt service documented by Auditor-General
summary: Chinese state media published coordinated articles dismissing Kenya's Standard Gauge Railway debt concerns as a "Western debt trap myth." CGTN's "The reality behind the Chinese debt trap myth in Africa" and Xinhua columns frame legitimate Kenyan fiscal concerns as Western propaganda. However, Kenya's Auditor-General Nancy Gathungu revealed Kenya owes China Exim Bank $741M in principal, $222M interest, and $41M penalties for 2025-2026 — the country's largest bilateral creditor. Kenya defaulted on SGR loans in October 2022, incurring $10.8M penalties. The SGR still runs at a loss. Xinhua's Nairobi bureau (largest outside Beijing, 37 bureaus across Africa) distributes content through Nation Media Group reaching 28M social media followers. ISS Africa documented how China offers "self-serving training to African reporters" as part of broader narrative control.
escalation_outcome: Undermining domestic fiscal accountability debate through foreign state media

headline: "They are coming to pick our boys": Post-Gen Z police fear narrative in Voi
summary: TAITA-006-006: "Since the Gen Z protests, we've seen a narrative that the police are enemies of the people. Some youths were shot dead during the protest here in Voi. People share photos of police trucks and say, 'They are coming to pick our boys.' This creates a permanent state of fear."
escalation_outcome: Permanent state of fear; community avoidance of police

headline: Manipulated video falsely shows Putin declaring support for Kenya Gen-Z protests; AI tools used to fabricate Russian connections and discredit protesters
summary: During Kenya's June-July 2024 Gen-Z protests, a manipulated video circulated claiming Putin supported the protests. Africa Check verified the video used genuine Putin footage overlaid with false English subtitles about patriotism and Ukraine — with no actual mention of Kenya. Separately, DFRLab documented that AI tools were used both to discredit protesters AND fabricate claims of Russian involvement (#TruthlessMan). Pro-government trolls claimed Russian interference to delegitimize protesters while pro-Russian accounts claimed solidarity to build influence — both exploiting the same crisis. Amnesty International documented state-paid troll networks (25,000-50,000 KES/day) creating counter-hashtags (#RutoMustGoOn against #RutoMustGo) and framing youth organizers as "traitors, foreign agents or mercenaries."
escalation_outcome: Erosion of trust in information during political crisis

There are people who are unable to distinguish whether such AI generated images are true or false." Documented state response: "majority of those who shared the image were arrested with some of them mysteriously disappearing to date."
escalation_outcome: Arrests; mysterious disappearances of sharers

This information which was not true caused some youths to mobilize and start protesting towards state-house." headline: "Thank you Putin for securing Christianity in Kenya": Russian-linked campaign frames Russia as protector of African values against Western cultural imperialism
summary: Africa Center for Strategic Studies documented Russian-linked social media campaigns in Kenya using religious framing to build pro-Kremlin sentiment. The narrative "Thank you Putin for securing the fate of Christianity in Kenya" was promoted through coordinated accounts, positioning Russia as defender of African Christian values against Western liberal cultural imperialism — specifically framing Western LGBTQ+ rights advocacy as an attack on African traditions. More than 70% of survey respondents in African countries believed Russian disinformation narratives including "Sanctions against Russia are the main cause for the current food and energy crises" were probably or definitely true. In digitally connected Kenya, pro-Russian messages are disseminated through networks of fake accounts, bots, and recruited local influencers under themes of national pride, often without disclosing Russian connections.
escalation_outcome: Deepening social divisions exploiting religious identity for geopolitical gain

headline: "Wantam" vs. "Tutam" ethnic political rhetoric dividing Kenya along tribal lines
summary: NAIROBI-047-003: "The dominant harmful messaging is about 'wantam' (one-term) and 'tutam' (two-term) political rhetoric driven by politicians. This narrative has divided the country along ethnic lines. If you are Kikuyu, people assume you are following Gachagua and you are pushing 'wantam'. If one is Kalenjin, they are presumed 'tutam'." The narrative has generated gang formation and geographic no-go zones: "Here in Kamkunji, there are places you cannot even mention 'Wantam'."
escalation_outcome: Armed gangs; geographic no-go zones; church attacks

Regions where the President did not get votes are regarded as 'non-shareholders.' This rhetoric has made people from some regions feel excluded from receiving government services." headline: Mogadishu Evictions-to-Recruitment Pipeline: Al-Shabaab frames evictions as "criminal acts"
summary: Al-Shabaab exploits FGS evictions in Mogadishu neighborhoods, framing 2024 public land reclamation as "criminal acts against the people" to delegitimize the government and fuel youth recruitment. Researcher matrix identifies CRITICAL risk with escalation pathway: eviction grievance → AS propaganda framing → youth radicalization → recruitment uptick in Banaadir.
escalation_outcome: Documented recruitment pipeline from policy grievance to VE radicalization

headline: Forbidden Stories leak: SVR-directed "The Company" ran €7.3M disinformation operation across Africa with fabricated journalist identities
summary: Forbidden Stories consortium analysed 76 leaked documents from "The Company," a Russian disinformation network directed by Russia's Foreign Intelligence Service (SVR), with a €7.3 million budget over 10 months (January-October 2024). The operation used fabricated journalist identities ("ghost reporters") to publish anti-French, anti-Western, and pro-Russian articles in local African media outlets. Narratives included: Western democracies as neo-colonial exploiters, Russia as defender of African sovereignty, and framing Western health/development programmes as surveillance tools. The operation recruited local influencers and manufactured grassroots-appearing content. ECFR documented this as part of Russia's broader hybrid warfare using "an ecosystem of hundreds of Russia-linked accounts and websites" with two well-known spreaders alone reaching 28 million followers.
escalation_outcome: State-sponsored covert influence infrastructure undermining media integrity

headline: False claims that NISA intelligence headquarters was attacked by Al-Shabaab in March 2024; no evidence found
summary: Widespread claims circulated on Twitter/X and through media reports that Al-Shabaab militants attacked the headquarters of Somalia's National Intelligence and Security Agency (NISA) on March 9, 2024. Africa Check investigated and found no credible evidence for the attack. The false claim appeared designed to either exaggerate Al-Shabaab's operational reach into the heart of Mogadishu's security infrastructure or to undermine confidence in NISA's ability to protect its own facilities. Such fabricated attack claims are a recurring pattern in Somalia's information environment, where verification capacity is limited and VE groups benefit from amplified fear narratives.
escalation_outcome: Erosion of confidence in security apparatus; amplification of VE threat perception

headline: African Initiative promotes anti-malaria vaccine disinformation in East Africa, claiming Western vaccines damage immune systems
summary: Russian intelligence-linked outlet African Initiative (run by Artem Kureev, connected to Russian intelligence services) launched a disinformation campaign targeting Western health initiatives in East Africa, specifically the RTS,S malaria vaccine programme. The narrative claims Western-developed vaccines damage African recipients' immune systems and that US-funded anti-malaria programmes serve as cover for bio-surveillance. WhatsApp messages with links to fake news stories were distributed in East African countries. The US State Department identified this as one of African Initiative's first major campaigns, calling it "deadly disinformation" that "puts lives at risk." Africa Center documented Russia as responsible for ~40% of disinformation content across Africa.
escalation_outcome: Direct threat to public health uptake of life-saving interventions

headline: London Mayor claims deepfakes could swing future UK elections
summary: A TikTok account is spreading content featuring London Mayor Sadiq Khan discussing concerns about deepfakes potentially influencing UK elections, referencing his own experience as a victim of AI-generated fake audio before Armistice Day protests. The content appears to be leveraging UK political concerns for potential disinformation purposes in East African contexts.

headline: Intergenerational ethnic stereotypes: "Wakikuyu ni Wakora," "Jokabimbe" and other dehumanizing labels
summary: NAIROBI-047-007: "In childhood, parents tell their children not to trust individuals from certain tribes: Kikuyus are conmen (Wakikuyu ni Wakora), Coast people are lazy (Wacoast ni wazembe), Somalis are violent (Wasomali wanapenda vita), Kamba engage in witchcraft (Wakamba ni wachawi)." Politicians then affirm: "Luos referred to as uncircumcised (Wasio tahiriwa), stone throwers (Watu wa kurusha mawe), railway uprooters (Watu wakung'oa reli). Kikuyus called thieves, apes (Jokabimbe)."
escalation_outcome: Embedded ethnic distrust; electoral violence risk
